data.title: Micron Stock: Why I'm Doubling Down
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc.'s (MU) wild ride has continued the last few weeks. Shares spiked to all-time highs leading up to a highly anticipated earnings report, only to fall precipitously the last few days. There are a few headwinds coalescing simultaneously that are causing this downturn, but I think the market reaction is overblown and that the stock will get back to its march higher in short order.

In my last piece on MU back in late January, I discussed how the memory maker was grappling with massive demand, why this shortage has been particularly pronounced, and the outsized profits that were cresting the horizon. The stock rose 15% over the next two months before crashing back down, now 20%+ off highs. That article can be read here.

This was just the most recent in a long series of articles that I've written on MU since initiating a Strong Buy in September 2024 (+286% since) and then downgrading to a Buy on November 2025 at around $200 per share, which I've maintained.

Throughout this run, I have seen many folks claim that the cyclical era of memory is over, that structural demand from AI will buoy the industry, and that "this time is different." I have even seen more than a couple references to Micron being "the next Nvidia." However, I have been adamant in my opinion that, despite the explosion in revenue and profit for Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung (SSNLF), memory remains as cyclical as ever. And my reasoning is simple: memory is cyclical because the markets it supplies are cyclical.

Smartphones, PCs, and processors all fall into this category. And yet, these products weren't always cyclical -- when they first burst onto the scene, the demand ceiling seemed impossibly high. Then, of course, competitors entered, technology became commoditized, and before anyone saw it coming, the market became oversupplied and prices crashed. So, the claim that AI isn't cyclical is myopic -- AI isn't cyclical because it hasn't been around long enough to see a downcycle. But that doesn't mean one isn't coming.

This may sound like semantics, but I think it's important to distinguish the difference because there are many investors who are piling into MU right now to catch the next NVDA, not realizing that, as has happened many times before, the stock is always in danger of a steep decline caused by a demand shock. Fixed costs in memory production are substantial and capacity expansion takes years to plan and complete, meaning Micron and its South Korean peers are about as far from mobile and agile as a company can be.

Therefore, I would posit that investing in Micron isn't about debating whether the memory manufacturing business is still cyclical -- it's about figuring out when the upcycle will turn into a downcycle. With that context now top of mind, let's jump into the company's most recent earnings report:

Going into the report, the market was expecting fireworks and Micron certainly delivered. Revenue of $23.9 billion was up 196% YoY and an outrageous 75% QoQ, gross margin ballooned to 74.9%, an increase of more than 1800 bps, and non-GAAP EPS smashed the company's record and came in at $12.20, 155% higher than the previous quarter.

While the memory shortages were driven by high bandwidth memory, or HBM, demand for AI data center build-outs, the ripples were felt across the industry at large, leading to an interesting dynamic:

While HBM revenues (counted within the Cloud Memory division) surged and gross margin got a boost to 74%, the difference is "only" about 800 bps QoQ. Looking at the company's other segments reveals even greater margin gains in the non-AI markets, with Core Data Center (OEM data center customers) matching the 74% gross margin for AI sales, and Mobile and Client (consumer division) exceeding that number by an additional 500 bps. Even Automotive and Embedded saw a substantial increase.

So HBM is driving the shortage, but from a volume perspective, the increases in DRAM and NAND prices are providing even greater benefits to Micron's non-AI business. Which makes sense -- HBM customers were already paying top dollar so the magnitude of the price changes is lower while products like DDR4, for example, are dirt-cheap to produce and are now selling for extremely elevated prices.

To capture some of the excess demand, Micron and the South Korean memory makers are picking up spending as the former committed $5 billion to capex in Q2 (while still managing nearly $7 billion in adjusted free cash flow). But as the shortage worsens, the Big 3 have remained slow to ramp capacity because decades of booms and busts have taught that the optimal path is to make gradual, measured moves to increase supply, even if demand reaches a fever pitch.

This wait-and-see approach will allow the industry to achieve a balance between supply and demand without crashing prices and risking another bust so soon after the previous one. And it will also allow them to make a mind-boggling amount of money. Micron provided the following guidance:

The company expects revenue of $33.5 billion at the midpoint (+260% YoY/+40% QoQ), gross margin around 81%, and non-GAAP EPS of $19.15 at the midpoint, which is +57% QoQ. To project these figures after what was already a historic quarter for Micron demonstrates just how dire this shortage is and just how persist demand has remained despite the higher prices. A gross margin of 81% is higher than Nvidia's (NVDA)! It's hard to imagine that just a few years ago (2023), Micron reported a negative gross margin in three consecutive quarters.

Okay, this earnings report just about covers the bull case for MU -- at the current run rate, the company might just earn $60 per share over the next four quarters. That's a P/E of about 6 by the way.

And yet, even with these results, investors opted to sell the news and the stock dropped by more than 8% the following trading day as profit taking overrode operating results. The selloff worsened following the outbreak of war in the Persian Gulf, leading to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, skyrocketing oil prices, and a flight to defensive stocks. As the market has moved towards de-risking, MU has been hit by yet more selling pressure as high-beta names are being shed in favor of safer ports.

Beyond macroeconomic pressure, there have been some negative headlines the last week or so, mainly regarding Google's (GOOG) new quantization algorithm TurboQuant, which claims to speed up the key-value ("KV") cache on AI processors by orders of magnitude. As of right now, an overwhelming majority of the memory on an AI processor is used for 1) the KV cache and 2) model weights, so the idea is that if the KV cache gets more efficient, chipmakers will need less memory to meet customer needs. In response, DRAM prices have fallen more than 5% this week as the market speculates on what this could mean for future demand (though the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and inflationary fears probably have something to do with the decline as well).

So, is this improvement in memory performance going to reduce overall bit demand and hurt Micron? To answer that question, we can look to real world examples. The obvious one is the origin of what's known as the Jevons paradox. In the second half of the 19th century, famed Scottish inventor James Watt invented a highly efficient steam engine, which greatly reduced the amount of coal necessary to achieve a given output of energy. Conventional wisdom at the time was that, as efficiency rose, coal consumption would therefore drop. However, the exact opposite occurred -- coal became cheaper and more accessible, which actually boosted overall demand long-term.

This concept is known as induced demand, which essentially means that, as the cost of a resource goes down, consumption goes up. A similar example was observed with the Katy Freeway, which stretches from western Texas all the way to Houston in eastern Texas. The state added new lanes aimed at reducing congestion in the early 2000s, but after a short-term decline, traffic became equal to or even worse than before the project.

Memory is certain to be in high demand for years to come as the hyperscalers have committed to hundreds of billions in AI capex. Cheaper memory won't convince them to be satisfied with their current plans -- it will induce even further demand as they all race to win the AI arms race. So, no, I don't think TurboQuant is a negative catalyst for MU, and I expect that we might even look back on this as a moment of democratization for memory that allows other players besides the largest tech companies, which currently account for nearly all of HBM shipments, to enter the game.

Investor Takeaway

Full disclosure, I was writing this article Tuesday night before the ~10% move higher on Wednesday but my thesis remains unchanged. I expect MU to continue to outperform in the short and medium-term as the current upcycle continues and even accelerates. Capacity expansion will continue to incrementally add to revenue and net income in the quarters ahead, even as a likely easing of sky-high prices and lower utilization (of new fabs) brings gross margins down from the 80% expected next quarter.

In the long-term, I expect MU to become the victim of another downcycle eventually as AI becomes cyclical like all the rest of the secular tech trends that came before it. I don't think this inflection is approaching any time soon, but owning this stock is an exercise in always keeping your ears and eyes open for the next shock. But for now, I like MU at current price levels and expect continued movement higher as the supercharged operating results come in and tensions in the Middle East ease.

As such, I am reiterating a Buy on MU.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: 10% Yield Worth Considering From Rithm Capital
data.description:
Rithm Capital (RITM) is a mortgage REIT that is more complex than many of its peers, but it has also delivered significantly better returns over long time periods. Its business model has simply worked very well. I own shares of the common stock and some of the preferred shares; specifically, I have positions in RITM-D (RITM.PR.D), RITM-E (RITM.PR.E), and RITM-F (RITM.PR.F) for the preferred shares.

The Preferred Share RITM-A (RITM.PR.A)

When I look at RITM-A, I see a share that is trading just under our "Buy" rating. Shares were trading at $24.88 when I prepared this article.

That results in about $0.42 of dividend accrual and puts the stripped price at about $24.46. The shares have an annualized yield to call of 35%.

For RITM-A, the attractive feature is the floating yield. Based on the current short-term rates, we see a floating yield a bit over 10%. That looks pretty good. That is a high enough yield that many investors would be fairly happy with the shares.

I am not expecting an immediate call, so we're not going to focus overwhelmingly on the yield to call. Instead, we will look at the floating yield on price. RITM-A, as of writing this article, had a floating yield on price of 10.10%. These shares are trading a bit under our buy range. I am not overwhelmingly excited about them, given there are other options right now, but I'm also certainly not in a bearish position at all. I would say this is a viable price for investors who want to initiate positions, though it falls short of being a no-brainer price.

When we think about shares like RITM-A, we aim to evaluate the floating spread of 5.802% plus the short-term secured funding rate. It's typical to still discuss it as being LIBOR, since for the purpose of these contracts, they are using SOFR plus 0.26161% as a replacement for three-month LIBOR. The original contracts all referenced three-month LIBOR.

Your Expectations

  • If you believe that short-term rates will remain near current levels, or if you believe they will increase modestly, then these shares would probably be quite attractive to you.

  • If you expected short-term rates to fall under 1%, then these shares would be significantly less attractive.

  • If you expected short-term rates to fall that far within the next few years, you would want to be focusing much more on utilizing bonds or using some fixed-rate preferred shares that trade at significant discounts to call value because of a lower coupon rate. You wouldn't want shares that trade at a significant discount to call value because the credit quality is garbage. That would not be a good choice if you were projecting the short-term rates to fall.

I am also using the standard logic that you would not expect the economy to be extremely strong with short-term rates plunging. Those don't usually go together.

Federal Reserve

The Federal Reserve has demonstrated a preference for higher rates. When inflation is not below their target, this has been demonstrated in speeches by referring to the increase in the interest rate on excess reserves as normalization. They called it normalization for years while preparing to raise rates and while raising rates. However, for the larger part of a decade (around 2009 to late 2016), the rate was about 0%. That was also the entire history for the interest rate on excess reserves at the time. In my opinion, if something was approximately 0% from the time it was created until eight years after it was created, it is not reasonable to refer to rapid increases as normalization. That is abusing the English language. You can't normalize something by making it materially different from every value it has ever held in the past.

Conclusion

Now, when we think about RITM-A, it's reasonable to say that the yield to call and stripped yield make these shares attractive. RITM-A’s price would need to climb a material amount for shares to have a negative yield to call. We see some preferred shares that trade at a negative yield to call. However, it is not particularly common. At the time that I am preparing this article, there were exactly three mortgage REIT preferred shares that had a negative yield to call. Those were NLY-F (NLY.PR.F) and DX-C (DX.PR.C). Therefore, I would not expect the returns on RITM-A to be materially higher than the stripped yield or the floating yield on price. However, that suggests that it would not be unreasonable for investors to see an annualized rate of return around 10%.

There is always a risk of the price dropping further, but the risk-adjusted return profile appears attractive. I don't believe there is a strong case for prices to plunge. That generally has not happened previously. We've seen some weakness during Liberation Day. There was a great deal of weakness during the pandemic. You may recall the entire market had a great deal of weakness during the pandemic. Therefore, I think it's reasonable to base our assessments on the other periods. Outside those two events, we haven't seen significant volatility.

data.title: The Small-Cap Rotation Is Here: How Income Investors Can Participate Through SCAP
data.description:
After years of playing second fiddle to mega-cap technology stocks, small-cap equities have staged a decisive breakout. The Russell 2000 Index* (RTY) surged 5.4% in January 2026, roughly quadrupling the S&P 500* Index’s (SP500) 1.4% gain over the same period.1 That January rally included a historic 14-session winning streak for the small-cap benchmark, the longest in nearly 30 years. Market observers have dubbed the shift the “Great Rotation,” and a growing body of evidence suggests it has further room to run.

For income-focused investors, the question is not whether to pay attention to small caps. The question is how to access this opportunity while still generating meaningful yield. The InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF (SCAP) offers one answer.

Why Small Caps, Why Now

The fundamental case for small-cap stocks rests on three pillars: valuation, monetary policy, and earnings momentum. On valuation alone, small caps trade at nearly a 31% discount to large caps on a forward price-to-earnings basis.2 That gap represents one of the widest valuation spreads in recent memory and has drawn comparisons to the early 2000s, when small-cap value dramatically outperformed following the dot-com bust.

The Federal Reserve’s rate cuts to 3.50–3.75% have lowered borrowing costs for smaller companies, which tend to carry more variable-rate debt than their larger peers.3 Falling short-term rates have also stabilized net interest margins for regional banks, a sector heavily represented across small-cap indices.

Earnings results have reinforced the narrative. Small-cap companies posted a 65% beat rate in Q4 2025.4 Franklin Templeton noted that “both small-cap quality and value are poised for meaningful rebounds in 2026.”5 Morningstar strategist Dave Sekera added that, based on current valuations, small caps “have further to run.”6

Inside SCAP: Active Small-Cap Income

The InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF launched in December 2023 with a straightforward mandate: deliver income and growth opportunities from an actively managed portfolio of approximately 80 small-cap stocks.7 The fund invests at least 80% of its assets in small-cap equity securities selected through a combination of quantitative screening and qualitative fundamental analysis, with a deliberate tilt toward value stocks that exhibit both income potential and earnings growth.

SCAP’s distribution history tells a compelling story. The fund has raised its quarterly per-share payout consistently since inception, climbing from $0.175 to $0.240. That most recent increase, a $0.035 jump in December 2025, brought the annualized distribution to $2.88 per share and the 30-day SEC yield to approximately 7.07% (as of 12/31/2025).

The fund’s top holdings reflect its value-and-income orientation. Preferred Bank (PFBC), StoneX Group (SNEX), Bank of N.T. Butterfield (NTB), and East West Bancorp (EWBC) anchor the portfolio with meaningful exposure to regional financials. NorthWestern Energy (NWE) and Chord Energy (CHRD) provide utility and energy sector diversification. No single position exceeds 3.6% of the portfolio, and the overall P/E ratio stands at 16.55, consistent with a disciplined value approach.

Performance metrics support the strategy. SCAP has delivered a 15.84% annualized return since inception (12/11/2023) and a 9.94% one-year return as of early March 2026, with a year-to-date gain of 4.54%. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The investment returns and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Combining Income and Small-Cap Exposure

The traditional knock against small-cap investing has been volatility without income. Most small-cap ETFs offer negligible yield, forcing income-seeking investors to choose between growth potential and cash flow. SCAP’s structure attempts to resolve that tension by pairing active stock selection in the small-cap universe with a distribution policy designed to grow over time.

This matters especially in the current environment. With small caps trading at a historic discount, rate policy turning favorable, and earnings trends strengthening, the asset class presents a window of opportunity. Investors who have historically avoided small caps due to a lack of income now have a vehicle designed to provide both.

The rotation into smaller companies may prove to be more than a momentary shift. If valuation gaps continue to compress and economic conditions remain supportive, portfolios with meaningful small-cap exposure could benefit for quarters to come. SCAP positions itself at the intersection of that opportunity and the need for dependable monthly income.

Notes

1. HCR Wealth Advisors, January 2026 Market Review; Clark Capital Management Group, January 2026 Benchmark Review.

2. Morningstar via MarketWatch, Four Reasons to Buy This Overlooked Group of Stocks, January 14, 2026; Easterly Funds, 2026 Outlook: Small Cap Value.

3. Federal Reserve Board, FOMC Statement, January 28, 2026; Federal Reserve, FOMC Minutes, December 10, 2025.

4. Bloomberg Intelligence, Small Caps Flourish as U.S. Earnings Growth Broadens Beyond Tech, February 2026.

5. Franklin Templeton (Royce Investment Partners), What’s Next for US Small-Caps in 2026, January 2026.

6. Morningstar, The Best ETFs to Ride the Small-Cap Stock Rally, Dave Sekera, January 29, 2026.

7. InfraCap Advisors, SCAP Fund Overview and Fact Sheet; StockAnalysis.com, SCAP ETF Overview.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Snapchat: Activist Calls For 'Year Of Efficiency' (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Snap Inc. (SNAP) has been the worst performer in the social media sector. AI has helped the stronger get stronger, furthering the gap to the smaller operators like SNAP. The fundamental picture isn’t exactly pretty here, but the balance sheet is reasonable and the valuation is deeply distressed. The involvement of an activist investor might be the spark needed to shift the focus towards profitability, which I expect to be the main catalyst to hope for. I upgrade the stock to a Strong Buy rating.

SNAP Stock Price

I last covered SNAP in January, where I stuck by my Buy rating in spite of the deteriorating fundamentals. The stock has been demolished since.

In hindsight, the “low valuation” argument clearly did not offer much in the way of downside support. Call it conviction, call it stubbornness, but I remain of the view that this is no time to give up in spite of the hazy outlook ahead.

SNAP Stock Key Metrics

SNAP is a social media company that is widely known for being popular with younger generations. At one point, many (including yours truly) viewed it to be a potential “Facebook-killer.” In other words, SNAP was like TikTok before that ever existed.

There is some irony in that the present state of affairs suggests something very different, in which both Facebook and TikTok are still dominant but SNAP is now the one in danger. In the most recent quarter, the company saw global daily active users decline sequentially. The only other time this occurred was all the way back in 2018 - at the time, this was attributed to a poorly received app redesign. That ended up to be a fixable issue but this time it might not be so clear given the likely competitive drivers behind it. SNAP saw its user losses primarily in North America, which is particularly concerning given that this is its most lucrative user base.

SNAP managed to offset the DAU weakness with growth in average revenue per user ("ARPU"), but I view user growth to be a more sustainable driver of long term trends.

The company ended up posting 10% YoY revenue growth. This is a solid result given the stock valuation, but pales in comparison to the far higher growth rate at Meta Platforms (META) in spite of the latter operating at a far larger scale.

There are some green shoots. The company has seen much of its revenue growth come from higher quality sources including its subscription offering, which drove more than 50% of the revenue growth in the quarter. That said, investors probably are not looking at this using a “glass half full” kind of mentality and are instead more focused on the lower nominal growth rate.

The company made some progress on profitability, with GAAP net income increasing slightly to $45 million, representing a 3% margin.

While that margin paled in comparison to the 50% margin seen at META’s social media businesses, it represented solid progress. The company generated a 48% GAAP incremental operating margin in the quarter and 45% incremental margin for the full year. Again, the company has lots of progress that it needs to make but these are solid gains given that the company is likely to be a beneficiary from AI efficiencies over the long term.

SNAP ended the quarter with $2.9 billion in cash versus $3.5 billion of debt. I note that the company has refinanced their convertible debt using “normal” debt and thus is now paying considerably more interest expense than in prior years.

Looking ahead, management guided the first quarter to see up to $1.53 billion in revenue, representing an acceleration to 12% YoY growth. Management notably has included more verbiage regarding goals towards profitability.

On the conference call, management noted their goal to “strike a better balance between the pace of community growth and the rate of top line growth” as they shift towards profitable growth. Management did notably discuss their belief that gross margins may move higher from here as a result of intentional changes in investment philosophy. Management estimated that “something like 40% of new code at Snap is AI generated” and I again note my expectations for the company to be able to improve their cost structure through AI. Social media stocks have been in the headlines as of late due to a landmark addiction ruling. Their conference call occurred prior to that ruling, but management got ahead of the trial by noting that the “global ad revenue from impressions served to users under the age of 18 … is not material.”

Is SNAP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SNAP has seen a recovery as of late, spurred on by reports that the activist investor Irenic has taken a stake. In their letter, Irenic makes a set of suggestions that include but are not limited to aggressively boosting profitability through headcount reductions and aggressive discipline on the spectacles development. Irenic goes on to give a potential price target of $26.37 per share in the bullish case.

I find that price target to be rather aggressive, but in general agree with the recommended steps. I should note that SNAP has all the typical markets of a clear activist candidate and I wouldn’t be surprised if other activists join suit. The stock recently traded at just 9x this year’s earnings. Of course some may point out that these are non-GAAP earnings estimates (recall that the GAAP net margin remains negative).

But that misses the overarching point. SNAP trades at just around 1x this year’s sales. That is a huge discount to the roughly 6x multiple at META (and I view the latter to be deeply undervalued).

Even if we assume that SNAP’s long term net margin opportunity is significantly lower than the current 50% GAAP operating margin at META’s advertising businesses, then SNAP stock still looks too cheap. Assuming just a 20% long term net margin, SNAP is trading right around 5.5x long term earnings. The balance sheet isn’t in too bad a shape, and with AI continuing to make advances, it is quite believable to think that SNAP could drive profit margins sharply higher if and when management chooses to do so.

At this point, my own price target stands at around 2x sales, implying 10x long term earnings. This is much lower than my prior target of 3x sales and that reduction is due to SNAP’s worsening competitive positioning relative to META. I expect that competitive positioning to continue to worsen given META’s ability to invest aggressively in AI. Even so, 2x sales implies strong upside for the stock.

The only question is that of the catalyst. The only catalyst I see is that of driving profit margins higher - this is direct and likely to immediately drive that multiple expansion, but investors are at the mercy of management. Co-founders CEO Spiegel and CTO Murphy collectively own an astounding 335 million shares, representing 20% of shares outstanding and essentially 100% of the voting power.

I should note that CEO Spiegel receives rather modest compensation from the company.

The last several years have shown that management’s firm control of voting power has hurt shareholders due to the focus on spectacles instead of driving advertising profits higher, but one should not underestimate the power of large insider ownership at play here. With management explicitly signaling a commitment to profitable growth even prior to the announced involvement of this activist investor, I expect the profitability catalyst to come sooner than later.

SNAP Stock Key Risks

It is possible that SNAP is in the process of being disrupted by Meta Platforms and others. It is possible that the company is unable to drive GAAP margins higher as growth slows or even turns negative. It is possible that management does not engage with the activist or pursue any of the suggested ideas. It is possible that the stock does not perform well until management more urgently drives profitability higher.

SNAP Stock Conclusion

SNAP looks poised to see struggling top-line growth as META and others boost their dominance through AI. Yet my bullishness for the stock has grown largely due to the demolished-valuation. I expect the company to drive margin expansion which would directly address the relative discount to peers. SNAP is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I upgrade the stock to a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Transmission Channels Of The War On Iran To The Brazilian Economy
data.description:
We are now in the fifth week since the U.S. airstrike that killed top leaders of the Iranian regime, initiating a war involving the United States and Israel against the country. More than a month of mutual bombardments between Iran and Israel has ensued, extending to other Persian Gulf nations, U.S. military installations—and even Cyprus. From a global perspective, the impact has stemmed primarily from disruptions to regional production of goods and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

An impasse currently prevails, suggesting a prolongation and probable intensification of the war: while U.S. troops are converging for potential air and naval invasions—and Trump, on March 26, extended his deadline to bomb Iran’s power plants to April 6—the Iranians have vowed to assert sovereignty over the Strait and impose heavy tolls on traffic passing through it. It is no coincidence that Fatih Birol—head of the International Energy Agency (IEA)—stated that a war with Iran poses the greatest threat to global energy "in history."

The channels through which this war impacts the Brazilian economy are manifold. On March 27, the price of Brent crude oil briefly surpassed US$110 per barrel. While this development improves the country's terms of trade—given that it is now a net oil exporter (Figure 1)—this price surge has already begun to drive up domestic costs for gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel, thereby increasing transportation, logistics, and airfare costs and fueling inflation in the short term. Additionally, it is worth highlighting the country's dependence on imported fertilizers.

The OECD’s Interim Economic Outlook 2026 report, released this week, indicates a slight downward revision in its GDP growth projections for Brazil for 2026 and 2027—dropping from 1.7% to 1.5% this year and from 2.2% to 2.1% next year—compared to the report issued in December. The overall trade balance is expected to benefit from rising prices for crude oil exports. Conversely, it is worth noting the negative, albeit less significant, impact that freight and other costs will have on other components of the current account of the balance of payments.

Regarding trade with Middle Eastern countries affected by the war, Brazil exports goods amounting to approximately 0.6% of its GDP to the region, while imports account for roughly 0.3% of GDP. Certain specific sectors will suffer heavier impacts, such as corn exports, 25% of which are destined for the Middle East, according to Cassiana Fernandez, Mirella Mirandola Sampaio, and Vinicius Moreira. “Brazil: Economic Outlook after the Oil Shock,” JPMorgan, Global Economic Research, 19 March 2026. It is also important to highlight that 15% of Brazil's fertilizer imports originate from the war-affected region—fertilizers with properties that render them non-substitutable by those from other sources, such as those imported from Morocco.

Tax revenue is expected to rise, reflecting the increase in oil exports. The Ministry of Finance estimates that a Brent crude price of US$100 per barrel would result in an increase of nearly 1% of GDP in additional revenue relative to the 2026 budgetary projections.

At the same time—as in many other countries—fiscal measures have been implemented to mitigate the effects of rising diesel prices. On March 12, the government eliminated federal taxes on diesel (PIS/Cofins), imposed a 12% levy on crude oil exports, and established a 50% tax on diesel exports, in addition to launching a direct subsidy of 0.32 reais per liter (equivalent to 6% of the price of 5.42 reais per liter on March 1), effective until December 2026. Analyses by industry experts to which I have had access suggest that this package—already in force via provisional measures and awaiting parliamentary approval—tends to be fiscally neutral.

Although Brazil has become the world's sixth-largest oil exporter and possesses the globe's eighth-largest refining complex—according to Eurasia Group (2026)—the country still imports 25% of the diesel it consumes. Given the critical role this fuel plays in freight transport in the country (Figure 2) and in electricity generation across much of the Amazon region—where three million people rely on isolated diesel-powered systems—there is naturally concern regarding the impact of its price and availability should the war be prolonged. Beyond the damage already inflicted on production facilities in the war-affected region, there is also the fact that transit via the Strait of Hormuz lacks sufficient alternative transport routes in the short term.

In a scenario where the war is prolonged, the issue of fertilizer dependency becomes an even greater cause for concern. Most Brazilian soil is naturally nutrient-poor, necessitating the use of fertilizers to sustain high crop yields. According to Eurasia Group (2026), fertilizers can account for as much as 34% of effective operational costs for summer corn, 30% for wheat, and 27% for soybeans and second-crop corn. Most of the country's current crop is already in the harvest phase, while producers have until roughly September to secure inputs for the next planting cycle.

Brazil currently accounts for 60% of global soybean exports and is also the world's largest producer and exporter of beef, among other food products. However, it is also the world's largest importer of fertilizers, relying heavily on imports for all three primary nutrients. Figure 3 depicts the weight of fertilizer imports relative to domestic production levels.

Iran, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and the United Arab Emirates accounted for 36% of Brazil's urea imports in 2025—shipments that are currently blocked due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Urea prices in Brazil surged by 35% in the first weeks of March and are likely to rise further as long as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed (Eurasia Group, 2026). Exposure to phosphates is also significant: 78% of Brazil's phosphate consumption is imported, and China—historically a key alternative supplier—has restricted exports to safeguard its domestic food security since the onset of the conflict.

If access to fertilizers remains restricted through September, Brazil faces a high risk of shortages and rising domestic prices—potentially as early as the 2026/2027 crop season—as highlighted in technical notes produced by the Executive Secretariat of the Ministry of Agriculture and reported by Folha de S. Paulo. These notes point to a potential phosphate deficit of between 1 and 3 million tons—equivalent to approximately 20% of Brazil's total demand.

There are also monetary and financial transmission channels through which the war impacts Brazil. The prospect of worsening inflation has already prompted the Central Bank to slow the pace of its interest-rate reductions, opting for a cut of just 25 basis points—to 14.75%—this month. One must also consider the trajectory of the U.S. dollar relative to other currencies, including the real.

Financial conditions abroad have deteriorated significantly. Global stocks and bonds suffered their largest combined sell-off this month since 2022, as the energy shock triggered by the war in Iran left investors with "nowhere to hide"—to borrow a phrase from Emily Herbert in the Financial Times. In the U.S., Treasury yields have fluctuated within a wide range on many days since the war began, as investors reassess the extent to which rising oil prices will feed into inflation and affect the Federal Reserve’s outlook on interest rates.

Strictly speaking, however, for the Brazilian economy, the greatest concern centers on diesel—and, above all, on fertilizers.

Original post

Otaviano Canuto, based in Washington, D.C., is a former vice president and a former executive director at the World Bank, a former executive director at the International Monetary Fund, and a former vice president at the Inter-American Development Bank. He is also a former deputy minister for international affairs at Brazil’s Ministry of Finance and a former professor of economics at the University of São Paulo and the University of Campinas, Brazil. Currently, he is a senior fellow at the Policy Center for the New South, a professorial lecturer of international affairs at the Elliott School of International Affairs—George Washington University, a nonresident senior fellow at the Brookings Institution, a professor affiliate at UM6P, and principal at the Center for Macroeconomics and Development.

data.title: United Therapeutics: Maintaining 'Strong Buy' On Second TETON Study Program Win
data.description:
The last time I wrote about United Therapeutics Corporation (UTHR) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "United Therapeutics: Nebulized Tyvaso Label Expansion Possible For IPF After Study Win." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was able to achieve positive results from its phase 3 TETON-2 study using nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. Specifically, the primary endpoint of change in absolute Forced Vital Capacity [FVC] from baseline to week 52 was met in favor of Tyvaso in a statistically significant manner. I had a Strong Buy rating, and today I'm going to stick to it because of what just happened.

The company was able to achieve another win with respect to the development of this IPF treatment program. A few days ago on March 30, 2026, it noted that it had also achieved positive results from its phase 3 TETON-1 study using Tyvaso to treat patients with IPF. What makes this study a huge win compared to the last one is that there was an even better effect on improving absolute [FVC] in this trial. Plus, this study was the one that recruited patients from the United States and Canada. Why the continued Strong Buy rating? That's because now this company has positive data from both TETON-1 and TETON-2 to seek supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] approval of Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF.

Speaking of which, as an update, it believes it is on track to submit such a regulatory application for approval of this IPF indication by the end of this summer. Even better, it is going to seek Priority Review as well, which could speed up the review time. Lastly, the company is already in the process of an expansion opportunity. It is also currently evaluating the use of Tyvaso in the ongoing phase 3 TETON-PPF for the treatment of patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis [PPF]. With its second phase 3 study win of Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF, plus an sNDA of this drug for the treatment of these patients on track for the end of this summer, I believe that it is important to maintain a Strong Buy rating for this stock.

Second Phase 3 Study Win Reinforces Continued Bullish View For IPF Program Going Forward

As I noted above, United Therapeutics already had positive data from its phase 3 TETON-2 study using nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF. I went over this study the last time around. What's even more impressive is that the second phase 3 study, TETON-1, not only achieved the primary endpoint of FVC but exceeded what was observed in TETON-2. I believe that it is important to go over this other study that had achieved positive results. Before going over the phase 3 TETON-2 study in detail, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Pulmonary Fibrosis [PF] is characterized as a progressive lung disease where both scarring and hardening occur. As you can imagine, damage to the lungs makes it hard to breathe, which is one problem. The other issue stems from the fact that the lung itself is then unable to transfer oxygen to the blood stream as well. The point is that as the disease progresses rapidly, it can lead to respiratory failure and/or death. Plus, it is not clear what exactly causes the scarring of the liver. The truth is that it could be a variety of factors that are not entirely clear, such as autoimmune disorders, inhaled toxins, viral infections, gastroesophageal reflux disease [GERD], and others.

However, the truth is that the cause of the fibrosis [scarring] is typically not known. In this sense, this is known as Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis [IPF]. With that being said, these PF patients experience some symptoms, such as:

  • Dry Cough
  • Chest pain
  • Shortness of breath
  • Feeling faint.

If that isn't bad enough, this disease can progress rapidly, and this is an issue because the damage that occurs to the lungs of a patient is irreversible. What makes this drug an ideal one is that it is able to accomplish two things, which are improved lung function and an antifibrotic effect. This is thanks to some key mechanisms that Tyvaso [inhaled treprostinil] deploys. First and foremost, there is the ability for this drug to have an antifibrotic effect, and this involves two possible items:

  • Reduction of lung inflammation and fibrosis thanks to activating EP2 and DP1 receptors, which in turn inhibit the formation of fibrotic tissue.
  • Reduction of fibrosis due to inhibition of smooth muscle cell proliferation and myofibroblasts - point of this is to reduce collagen formation that leads to fibrosis.

Another action of Tyvaso is to reduce such inflammatory signaling pathways of lung macrophages as NF-κB and MAPK, thereby inhibiting inflammation of the lung. Lastly, IPF causes a major issue known as ventilation perfusion mismatch. What does this even mean? Well, this means that there are decreased oxygen levels thanks to poorly circulated parts of the lungs. In turn, this results in hypoxia [where tissues do not receive enough oxygen]. Thus, another mechanism of action [MOA] of Tyvaso is to act as a vasodilator and improve ventilation-perfusion mismatch.

The global idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis market is expected to reach $6.7 billion by 2035. The reason why the company is in good shape to go after this market is because, despite available therapies, it could set a new standard of care [SOC] option for these patients. How so? This is thanks to several reasons.

First and foremost, the unprecedented ability to obtain statistical significance in several endpoints in clinical testing is not achieved by other IPF drugs. Secondly, current therapies for these patients not only offer up modest clinical benefits but also have significant side effects. Lastly, there is a uniqueness to Tyvaso, which is that it is inhaled directly into the lungs. In addition to having multimodal activity not observed in other IPF therapies like targeting fibrotic, vascular, and inflammatory pathways. The best part about this drug is that it has the potential to be the first and only inhaled anti-fibrotic treatment for these IPF patients.

In order for the company to establish the efficacy of Tyvaso, it had to evaluate it as part of the TETON study program. As I mentioned beforehand, the company indeed was able to meet the primary endpoint of the phase 3 TETON-2 study back in September of 2025. It noted that patients that took its drug Tyvaso were able to achieve a statistically significant FVC improvement of 95.6 mL from baseline compared to placebo, with a statistically significant p-value of p<.0001 [United Therapeutic Corporate Presentation Phase 3 TETON-2 Results September 28, 2025, slide #11]. This type of improvement in FVC in itself was impressive.

However, I also mentioned that United was gearing up to release results from its other phase 3 TETON-1 study. This clinical study was the one that recruited a total of 598 patients in the United States and Canada and then randomized them 1:1 to receive either of the following treatments over a 52-week period:

  • Nebulized Tyvaso
  • Placebo.

The primary endpoint set out was the change in Forced Vital Capacity [FVC] from baseline between the drug and the placebo. This is where an even better win came from in terms of this efficacy endpoint measure. This time around, patients achieved an absolute change in FVC of 130.1 mL. Not only did this end up achieving statistical significance with a p-value of p<0.001, but as you can see, it is an even greater difference compared to TETON-2, where absolute FVC ended up being an improvement of 95.6 mL. The beauty of this program is the achievement of a successful outcome of the phase 3 TETON-1 study, but even better, this provides the potential to move this program forward. It is expected that the company is going to submit an sNDA of Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF by the end of this summer. While this catalyst is a bit far away, there is another more near-term one to consider in the meantime, and this would be a presentation of data. United Therapeutics expects to present additional TETON-1 results and integrated analyses of TETON-1 and TETON-2 data at the American Thoracic Society Annual Meeting in Orlando in May 2026.

There is an expansion opportunity underway, and this is the evaluation of the ongoing phase 3 TETON-PPF study. This late-stage trial is exploring the use of nebulized Tyvaso to treat patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis [PPF]. The reason why United was able to advance this PPF treatment program and TETON-1/TETON-2 was thanks to the prior post-hoc analysis data observed in patients with pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease [PH-ILD] from the INCREASED study. It was noted that the drug was able to achieve significant improvement in FVC.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, United Therapeutics had $4.7 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable investments as of December 31, 2025. The thing about this company is that it is generating a considerable amount of sales for Tyvaso. In Q4 of 2025, it generated revenues of $464 million for both Tyvaso DPI and Nebulized Tyvaso, which ended up being a year-over-year growth in Q4 of 2025 of 12% [United Therapeutics Corporate Presentation Fourth Quarter 2025 Corporate Update, slide #8].

However, such growth was even larger when looking at the entire year period instead. Total Tyvaso revenues in 2025 grew by 16% to $1.878 billion, compared to the prior year, where sales only reached $1.62 billion. I will state that it accomplished a huge feat for 2025, which is that for the first time it surpassed $3 billion in revenue. The downside is that Q4 of 2025 revenues of $790.2 million missed analysis expectations. However, this still ended up being a sales increase of 7% year over year. In terms of its earnings per share [EPS] of $7.70, it not only beat analysts' expectations of $6.76–$6.78 but also grew by 24% year over year.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in United Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive results that were obtained from the phase 3 TETON-1 study using nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. In the more recent primary analysis efficacy endpoint of change in absolute FVC, the improvement of patients who took Tyvaso was observed to be 130.1 mL. This is much higher than that observed in the phase 3 TETON-2 trial. The company expects to submit an sNDA of Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF by the end of summer.

The risk here is that even though the primary efficacy endpoints were met in a statistically significant manner, there is no guarantee that the sNDA itself will be accepted for starters. Nor that the company will end up receiving U.S. marketing approval of Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of these IPF patients. In addition, the hope is to seek Priority Review Designation. Even if the FDA decides to accept the sNDA of this therapy for the treatment of these patients for review, there is no guarantee that Priority Review will be granted.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the financials I mentioned above. The company did very well, as I noted above, having generated full-year 2025 revenues of $1.878 billion, which ended up being a 16% year over year increase. The main growth driver for the company was that of Tyvaso, which grew by 12%. The risk here is that if sales of Tyvaso start to drop off, then that would not only mean lower revenues to be reported for Q1 of 2026, but it could also decrease full-year 2026 revenues as well.

The good news is that the company is looking to grow its Nebulized Tyvaso revenues with the sNDA for it for the treatment of patients with IPF. The problem is that there is no assurance that the company will indeed receive U.S. marketing approval for it. If it doesn't, then it will lose out on the possibility of expanding its drug to the multibillion-dollar IPF market.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity that United Therapeutics is working on. It is in the process of evaluating the use of Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis [PPF] in the ongoing phase 3 TETON-PPF study.

The risk here is that even though this drug has been shown to improve FVC in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis in both of the TETON-1 and TETON-2 studies, there is no guarantee that it will be able to do the same in TETON-PPF. The hope is that the company can continue to expand the use of Tyvaso for several other pulmonary indications, but there is no assurance that this will end up happening.

Conclusion

United Therapeutics has delivered a great amount of shareholder value since I last wrote about it. I had a Strong Buy rating then, and the stock has since gained 54.79%. I believe that there is still plenty of more upside for this name, which is one reason for maintaining my rating.

Another reason would be the win it just achieved a few days ago, which is the meeting of the primary endpoint of change in absolute FVC from baseline to week 52 in the phase 3 TETON-1 study. With this company achieving the primary endpoint in both of the phase 3 TETON studies, it is now going to be able to file an sNDA of Nebulized Tyvaso to the FDA for the treatment of patients with IPF.

Not only that, but as I mentioned above, the company is already evaluating the use of this drug in an expansion opportunity. This would be the ongoing evaluating of Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis [PPF] in the ongoing phase 3 TETON-PPF study. With positive data from the phase 3 TETON-1 study using Nebulized Tyvaso for the treatment of patients with IPF, plus the expected sNDA filing for this program by the end of summer 2026, I believe that this stock should remain a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Healthcare Exposure Focused On Big Pharma? You're Missing Out
data.description:
By Rafael Silva

The healthcare sector is undergoing a profound transformation, driven by rapid technological advancements and shifting global demographics. The leaders of this transformation go far beyond the big pharmaceutical names that dominate healthcare strategies. For investors seeking targeted exposure to this evolution, the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation Index (HTEC) provides a specialized, research-driven benchmark.

Investing in Solutions, Not Just Sectors

The index is designed to capture the entire value chain of disruptive and emerging healthcare technologies. It does this by moving away from traditional, broad sector classifications like “pharmaceuticals” or “healthcare providers,” and instead utilizing forward-looking subsegments to cover massive structural trends, from breakthroughs in new drug development to the systemic challenges of an aging population.

Ultimately, the process is a bottoms-up approach. The team identifies societal needs, determines what innovation is needed to address them, and that informs the creation of the subsegments, which are the actual fields able to provide the required solutions.

9 Key Subsegments Solving Critical Healthcare Challenges

Take the aging population as an example of this methodology in action. The latest U.S. Census projects that by the mid-2030s, older adults will outnumber children for the first time in U.S. history. As the global population ages, the strain on the healthcare system increases alongside physician shortages. Notably, the AAMC projects a shortfall of up to 86,000 physicians by 2036. HTEC captures solutions to this specific structural trend by including companies in segments like Robotics (surgical robots that bring improved outcomes and shorter recovery times), Medical Instruments (devices for chronic illnesses), and Telehealth (expanding access).

Healthcare Technology: From Luxury to Necessity

There’s a realization that healthcare technology is transitioning from a luxury to an absolute necessity. Challenges like unsustainably growing healthcare costs require technology to be properly addressed. U.S. national health expenditures were projected to reach $5.6 trillion in 2025 and $8.6 trillion by 2033, growing at nearly 5.8% annually and outpacing GDP growth. Technologies like AI diagnostics, telehealth, and robotics are uniquely positioned to solve these structural issues by increasing efficiency and drastically lowering costs.

But at the end of the day, investors care about strong balance sheets and execution. Recent years have seen a combination of important regulatory approvals in areas like precision medicine and genomics, alongside massive leaps in technology supporting AI-driven drug discovery. This, coupled with significantly reduced concerns regarding political regulations, has allowed companies to concentrate on executing their strategies and bringing treatments to the population.

However, because this space is evolving so rapidly, there is a widening gap between the true innovators and the legacy companies lagging behind. Identifying the companies with the technological edge and execution capabilities to actually deliver these treatments requires deep, specialized research. A passive approach simply won’t catch them in time.

Under the Hood: Constituent Selection and Scoring

To capitalize on these trends, a passive, set-it-and-forget-it approach is rarely enough. Healthcare innovation moves incredibly fast. For that reason, the HTEC index methodology is built specifically to stay agile, using a blend of quantitative metrics and deep qualitative research to select constituents. To be eligible for inclusion, a company must have a market cap above $200 million and a trailing 3-month average daily trading volume (ADTV) of at least $2 million.

Each company is then individually analyzed and assigned a custom score ranging from 1 to 100. This score is based on the level of revenue a company receives from innovative healthcare technologies, a qualitative and quantitative assessment of the company’s competitive positioning, technological edge, and market leadership, as well as the level of investment that the firm is actively making in its technologies, which indicates its commitment to future growth.

A company must have an aggregate score of 50 or higher to be considered for inclusion. This score also underpins the modified equal-weight model of the index. Unlike typical market-cap-weighted strategies where the weight is dictated simply by the size of the company, this model ensures that the strategy allocates more weight to the leading companies with the highest exposure to the theme.

Dynamic Weighting and Portfolio Agility

The modified equal-weighting scheme also adjusts every quarter. This means that not only is the index able to add and drop companies, but it can also adjust its exposure to reflect the constituents’ revenue purity, investment levels, and their market and technological leadership. This way, the strategy is always ensuring that it is overweighting the leading companies while rapidly absorbing new market entrants (like recent IPOs) without waiting for an annual review.

On top of that, the team is constantly updating the roadmap to make sure that the subsegments are relevant, hold the best companies, and effectively capture the entire value chain. After all, one of the key benefits of investing in a research-based strategy like this is that it’s forward-looking. So, while there is the ability to make changes often, those changes are primarily made to reflect the future of healthcare, rather than simply reacting to short-term market signals.

Conclusion

Ultimately, investing in the future of healthcare requires more than just buying the biggest legacy names in a broad sector strategy. As demographic shifts and unsustainably rising costs force the medical industry to adapt, disruptive technology is no longer just a nice-to-have, it’s the only viable path forward. By relying on a dynamic, research-driven methodology rather than a static market-cap weighting, the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation Index (HTEC) ensures it captures the companies actively solving these systemic issues. For investors looking to gain true exposure to the innovations shaping tomorrow’s medical landscape, moving beyond traditional sector classifications is a necessity.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Joby Aviation Stock Is Getting Ready For Takeoff
data.description:
With Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY) being one of the leaders in the next frontier of transportation, I find it quite surprising myself that I haven't initiated coverage on the stock yet since my analysis is mostly tech focused. Today, I have finally decided to provide an analysis on the company and I believe it comes at an important time. As you can see in the stock price chart below, JOBY stock has fallen nearly 50% in the past six months. Below, I will review their most recent earnings reported in late February and also discuss some important recent developments.

Below, it is shown that commercialization is nearer than ever before. Certification progress is significant and they are set for a Dubai launch later this year. While there are some risks such as further dilution and some legal risks with relation to China, the valuation's steep contraction makes the current setup opportunistic. Therefore, I'm initiating Joby stock with a Buy rating today.

Certification Not Far Away

We will get to some financial results later in this article but at this point those numbers may be secondary in importance. Let's first take a look at regulatory and commercialization progress. As you can see above, Joby has come a long way in terms of the certification process with the FAA. The company has fully completed Stage 1 and 3 while Stage 2 is on the cusp of completion. Meanwhile for Stage 4, on the FAA side they stated that they saw record progress as there was an 18 point advance.

For this industry, regulations are one of the large hurdles to commercialization and in my view the relative speed at which they are clearing these hurdles should be encouraging for investors. In early to mid March, Joby took a large step forward. It was reported that testing had begun for their first FAA-conforming electric air taxi aircraft. This has been called a key milestone and so the company continues to inch towards passenger service in the United States.

Uber Collaboration

Joby is set to start passenger services in Dubai later this year as they already announced the first few vertiports there. However, the even better signal that commercialization is near is their collaboration with Uber (UBER). Uber Air powered by Joby is a deep integration as Uber users will be able to book air taxis right in the Uber App. On the right, an illustration of what this could look like is shown and you can see that it is likely to be a seamless experience for users.

In my opinion, the willingness of Uber to collaborate with Joby shows that commercialization is really just around the corner. For Joby, being able to partner with one of the titans of the passenger transport industry is a vote of confidence and should help them to expand and scale as their services continue to mature. All in all, I like what I'm seeing as the company seems set to move away from the R&D phase.

The Numbers

For 2025 Q4, Joby reported a massive YoY increase in revenues. While their Blade Urban Air Mobility acquisition contributed to this strength, there are other factors like their Pentagon contracts. Nonetheless, their reported $30.84 million for the top line beat expectations by $13.95 million and even if these numbers aren't the most important right now, that is still an encouraging sign. As another promising sign, you can see above that Joby has narrowed their net loss YoY. Looking forward, the company expects to see between $105 million to $115 million of revenues for full year 2026. Therefore, even their numbers suggest that commercialization is near underway now.

Not long ago, Joby announced both an equity offering and a debt offering to fund their operations and capital investments. Of course, this means dilution for shareholders and moving forward that is still a risk. In the near term, I believe investors need not be too worried. As you can see above, in Q4 Joby's operations burned through $153.17 million in cash while FCF was at -$166.98 million. Cash burn has accelerated in recent times as the company gears up for commercial service but as of the end of Q4, they have $1.41 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

With their cash position being quite strong after the capital raise, further need for capital may not happen in the near future if the current cash burn rate continues. However, there are signs that cash burn could increase. Moving into 2027, the company aims to double production to address the opportunity at hand and they have signed an agreement to acquire a production facility near Ohio that's more than 700,000 square feet. To me, this sounds like a considerable need for cash to enable commercialization and so further dilution is far from being off the table in my view.

Trump Administration Is Supportive

While the FAA certification process isn't complete yet, the Trump Administration seems very supportive of the eVTOL industry. It is always nice to have the government on your side and so Joby may see an accelerated timeline in the United States. In early March, it was reported that Joby, along with seven other firms, was selected to take part in the Department of Transportation's air taxi pilot program. As Amy Thielen of Seeking Alpha writes

Under Executive Order 14307, Unleashing American Drone Dominance, President Trump declared that it is the policy of the U.S. to accelerate the safe commercialization of UAS and fully integrate UAS into the national airspace system, as well as eVTOL and other AAM aircraft operations.

Legal Risks

In addition to the dilution risk that I discussed earlier, Joby may have some legal risks to deal with. Back in March, it was reported that rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) had filed a countersuit against the company. Archer is accusing Joby of defrauding the U.S. Government and hiding a relationship with China. In response, Joby attorney Alex Spiro stated that

Archer’s constant legal issues and flailing business operations have left it no choice but to resort to invented nonsensical theories. We will see them in court.

At this point, it's anyone's guess how this lawsuit will turn out. Whatever the ultimate outcome may be, these events introduce potential legal risks for Joby and that may play a part in weighing on the stock's performance. Funny enough, this countersuit was reportedly filed on the same day that the DOT pilot program selection was announced. It seems that competition is getting ugly.

Valuation Seems Opportunistic

With the company's revenue only now slowly trickling in, the P/B ratio is likely the only valuation multiple that makes sense for Joby currently. As you can see in the chart above, the ratio has receded significantly from the highs of 2025. At the current reading of just 5.856, the multiple is just modestly above the three year median. In my view, this shows that the valuation is opportunistic.

In this article, it was shown that Joby is now nearer to commercialization than ever. Significant FAA certification progress has been made and through the Uber collaboration, the company seems ready to start passenger flights, starting with Dubai later this year. Meanwhile, the Trump Administration's supportive stance should accelerate their domestic efforts. While there are risks such as dilution and the Archer Aviation lawsuit, I believe the sharp contraction in the valuation provides investors with a good margin for error right now.

While a P/B reading of 5.856 isn't the cheapest in the world, given that eVOTL is the future of transport and that Joby is a leader in this rapidly expanding market, I would say that the valuation shows the risk/reward is favorable for long term investors.

Takeaway

Joby stock has been punished in the past few months. Judging by the stock performance, you might have thought that the company's growth story was getting derailed either due to regulatory pressures or technological roadblocks. In reality, the company seems on track to deliver on their mission. While there are always risks especially for a relatively young company like Joby, the valuation slide makes this a very attractive buying opportunity in my view. The stock seems to be getting ready for takeoff and so I have decided to initiate with a Buy rating now.

data.title: A Rough March For Gold As The Leading Precious Metal Searches For A Bottom
data.description:
March was a month in which a significant geopolitical event caused one commodity to respond in line with historical trading patterns, while another did not. On February 28, 2026, U.S. and Israeli forces began a military operation in Iran that continued throughout March, with Iran retaliating throughout the Middle East. Crude oil rallied from below $60 in late 2025 and early 2026 to over $100 per barrel. Rising crude oil prices have increased global inflationary pressures, with many analysts calling for even higher prices if the hostilities continue and escalate. Meanwhile, stocks and bonds fell as a risk-off environment led markets to trade amid the constantly changing news cycle.

Gold is typically an inflation barometer, but a rising U.S. dollar, elevated U.S. interest rates, and the price action over the past months that included substantial speculative buying, caused gold prices to post a double-digit percentage decline in March. The last time gold’s price fell by more than 10% in a month was in September 2011.

Gold enters April searching for a bottom, but the trend over the past twenty-seven years suggests that the market may have reached its nadir in March.

Gold falls by over 11% in March 2026- The other precious metals post declines

While selling descended on the gold market on January 29, 2026, and reached a low on February 2, 2026, gold recovered by the end of February. March was a different story as gold prices fell by more than 10%.

The daily continuous COMEX gold futures chart shows that gold’s price reached a record high of $5,626.80 on January 29 and fell 21.4% to $4,423.20 per ounce on February 2. However, gold settled January at $4,745.10, a 9.3% gain from the December 2025 closing price. The leading precious metal settled at $5,247.90 per ounce at the end of February, a 10.6% gain for the month. On March 31, 2026, nearby gold futures settled at $4,647.60 per ounce, an 11.43% loss for the month.

The other precious metals trading on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX divisions posted double-digit percentage declines in March:

  • COMEX silver futures fell 19.7%.
  • NYMEX platinum futures declined by 18%.
  • NYMEX palladium futures moved 18.65% lower.

Despite gold’s 11.4% decline, it was the sector’s best performer in March.

Gold posted its 10th consecutive record high in Q1 2026

The end of March was also the end of the first quarter of 2026.

The quarterly continuous contract COMEX gold futures chart shows that gold posted its 10th consecutive quarterly new record high in Q1 2026. Despite gold’s 11.5% decline in March 2026, the leading precious metal still closed 7.41% higher than at the end of Q4 2025.

A bull market since the turn of this century

Gold’s bull market is nothing new, as it has made higher lows and higher highs since reaching a bottom at $252.80 per ounce in Q3 1999.

The quarterly chart dating back to the 1970s highlights the ascent of gold’s price from the 1999 low to the latest Q1 2026 high. Gold has moved over 22 times higher from the 1999 bottom to the 2026 peak.

Every correction has been a golden buying opportunity- The reasons for gold’s current correction

The long-term chart shows that over the past 27 years, every correction in gold’s price has been a buying opportunity, with new highs following.

Gold’s current price weakness follows a parabolic move in 2025 and January 2026 that more than doubled the price from the end of 2024. Gold’s rally attracted substantial investment and speculative buying during the rally.

Open interest is the total number of open long and short positions in a futures market. Open interest changes can provide clues about investor and trader activity.

The daily chart of COMEX June gold futures shows that total open interest fell by 33.2% from 541,033 contracts on January 22, before gold reached its latest record high, to 361,409 contracts on March 31 after gold prices declined. The open interest decline was likely due to substantial long liquidation as gold prices declined. In a futures market, falling prices and declining open interest are not necessarily a bearish sign.

Gold’s decline has cleaned out weaker market participants holding long positions, which could mean the March 23, 2026, low of $4,100 per ounce on the continuous COMEX futures contract could mark a significant bottom for the gold market if the trend of higher lows and higher highs continues.

The factors that support a continuation of the over two and a half decade rally include:

  • Central banks continue to add gold to reserves, validating gold’s growing role in the global financial system. At its peak, the value of gold reserves held by governments exceeded the value of euro and U.S. dollar reserve currency holdings.
  • While the U.S. dollar remains the world’s fiat reserve currency, de-dollarization because of sanctions, tariffs, and the bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers supports continued gold accumulation.
  • Gold is a rare metal that has a unique role as an industrial commodity, an ornamental attraction, and a financial asset.
  • Gold remains in a long-term bullish trend, and the trend is always a trader or investor’s best friend.

The bottom line is that gold may have formed another new bottom and technical support level at $4,100 per ounce in March 2026, with higher highs on the horizon. Gold will need to trade over $5,626.80 per ounce, close to $1,000 above the end of March closing level, to establish its 11th consecutive record quarterly high before the end of June 2026. Another quarterly new high is a tall order for the gold market, but it is not impossible in the current environment.

GLD is the leading gold ETF, but IAU and BAR also own physical gold bullion- Leave room to add on any further declines

The most direct route to invest in or take a speculative position in gold is through the physical market for bars or coins. However, physical gold purchases often involve premiums for buyers and discounts for sellers.

COMEX gold futures have a delivery mechanism but require margin and use unique regulated trading accounts. Three gold ETFs that purport to own physical gold bullion are:

The SPDR Gold Shares (GLD): The GLD ETF is the oldest and most successful commodity ETF, introduced in November 2004. At $430.29 per ounce at the end of Q1, GLD had $155.11 billion in assets under management. On average, GLD trades over 14.95 million shares per day and charges a 0.40% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for GLD include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum and liquidity categories. The management fee receives a B- grade, while the highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

The iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU): The IAU ETF was introduced in January 2005. At $88.16 per ounce at the end of Q1, IAU had $70.52 billion in assets under management. IAU trades an average of ~15.9 million shares per day and charges a 0.25% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for IAU include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum and liquidity categories. The management fee receives a B+ grade, slightly better than GLD’s due to IAU’s lower expense ratio. The highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

The Granite Shares Gold Trust (BAR): The BAR ETF began trading in August 2017. At $46.13 per ounce at the end of Q1, BAR had $1.59 billion in assets under management. BAR trades an average of ~522,000 shares per day and charges a 0.17% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for BAR include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum category. BAR’s lower assets under management and daily trading volumes receive an A- in the liquidity category, lower than the highly liquid GLD and IAU ETFs. The management fee receives an A- grade, which is better than GLD and IAU due to BAR’s lower expense ratio. The highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

GLD, IAU, and BAR all do an excellent job of tracking gold prices because they hold 100% of their AUM in physical gold. I remain bullish on gold’s prospects in early April 2026 and believe the $4,100 level could mark a significant price bottom. However, it is virtually impossible to pick bottoms in any market during corrective periods, and gold is no exception. While I rate gold and the GLD, IAU, and BAR ETFs a buy in early April 2026, I suggest leaving room to add if the current correction takes prices lower over the coming weeks and months.

data.title: HHH Stock: 38% Valuation Discount Vs. Negative Growth Forecast | 2-Minute Analysis

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2-Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this request. So let's throw two minutes on the clock and dive in.

Today we are diving into Howard Hughes Holdings, ticker symbol HHH. We're going to kick things off here with the Quant Rating system, which currently has a HOLD rating on this REIT. And we're going to dive into the metrics here in just a moment. Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha analysts, they currently have a HOLD in aggregate and that's from two analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days.

Now lastly, Wall Street analysts currently have a BUY rating on this stock, and that's three analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

So let's dive deeper. This is a $3.68 billion market capitalization REIT found within the real estate sector and the real estate development industry.

Let's jump on into the valuation grade currently a B+. First metric to highlight here is the price to the AFFO forward is currently 8.87 compared to the sector at 14.45. A nice 38.64% discount. Secondly, highlighting price-to-book ratio here for the trailing 12 months has been 0.97 compared to the sector at 1.47.

Now jumping over to the growth grade it is a D+. The revenue growth forward is unfortunately a negative 5.59% compared to the sector which is up 3.8%. And if we scroll up here a little bit you will see that the EPS diluted growth forward is -4.23% compared to the sector which is up 5.09%.

Jumping into profitability it is currently a C grade. Clocking in that gross profit margin trailing 12 months is 47.69% compared to the sector at 66.81%. And cash from operations is $462.37 million compared to the sector at $273.69 million. So HHH is outperforming the sector 68.94% on that cash from operations metrics.

Jumping into momentum it is currently a D grade. It’s been a rough year for HHH, down negative 17.46%, which compared to the sector is only down 5.51%. And even more recently on the 6-month and the 3-month price performance you’ll see that this REIT has taken quite a beating.

Jumping into the revisions grade here is currently an A- grade. That is zero up revisions and one down revision for earnings per share over the last three months, and zero up revisions and one down revision for revenue numbers over the last three months. So come on over here to Seeking Alpha, follow Howard Hughes Holdings here. You'll get all the free breaking news alerts. Obviously, people have been watching this stock for quite a while, Bill Ackman was involved at one point, still kind of is with Pershing Square. Keep an eye on this.

And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below. And don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2-Minute Analysis gets published.
